🏭 Warehouse Network
Installation Guide

Step-by-Step Instructions for First-Time Installers

📦 Meraki AR112 — Wall Rack Cabinet
🔋 APC Smart-UPS SRT 2200VA
🌐 Meraki MX68 — Router
🔀 Meraki MS125-48FP — Switch
📶 T-Mobile MG21 — 5G Backup
📡 Meraki MR57 x6 — Wi-Fi APs
📹 Meraki MV12W x18 — Cameras
🏷️ 6 APs  |  18 Cameras

Atlanta, GA  |  Version 2.0

How to Use This Guide

Read this first before starting any work.

You are completely new to this — that is okay.

This guide uses simple English. Every step tells you exactly what to do with your hands. Read each step fully before doing it. Do not skip any step. If a step says STOP and check — stop and check.

✅
CHECKA test to confirm the step worked — never skip this
⚠️
WATCH OUTA common mistake — read carefully before doing the step
💡
INFOHelpful background so you understand what you are doing and why
🎒
CARRYEverything you need to bring with you to that room
Two main parts:

PART 1 — DRY RUN: Set up and test everything in Closet 104 before mounting anything permanently. Nothing is bolted to walls yet.

PART 2 — ON FIELD: Install devices room by room across the building. Work one room at a time.

What Each Device Does

Read this before touching any hardware.

📦 Meraki AR112 — Wall Rack Cabinet
Metal cabinet that holds all rack devices. Bolts to wall in Closet 104. Everything lives inside this cabinet.
📍 Wall of Closet 104
🔋 APC Smart-UPS SRT 2200VA — Battery Backup
Large battery backup. If the building loses power, this keeps all devices running for 15–25 minutes. Without it, a power cut kills everything instantly.
📍 Inside rack cabinet
🌐 Meraki MX68 — Router (Internet Gateway)
The front door to the internet. Connects the building to AT&T fiber and manages the 5G backup. Also creates and separates the network zones (VLANs).
📍 Inside rack cabinet
🔀 Meraki MS125-48FP — Switch (Device Hub)
Connects all devices together. Sends power through the same cable that carries data — so cameras and Wi-Fi access points do not need a separate power plug at each location. One cable does two jobs.
📍 Inside rack cabinet
📶 T-Mobile MG21 — 5G Backup Internet
Backup internet using a SIM card (like a phone plan). When AT&T goes down, this takes over automatically within 40 seconds. No one needs to press anything.
📍 Inside rack cabinet
📡 Meraki MR57 x6 — Ceiling Wi-Fi Access Points
Round white discs that mount on the ceiling and create the wireless internet signal in the building. 3 cover the office area, 2 cover the warehouse, 1 covers the right side of the building.
📍 Ceilings throughout building
📹 Meraki MV12W x18 — Security Cameras
Security cameras with a 256 gigabyte hard drive built in — they record and store footage without needing a separate recording device. They work indoors and outdoors. Powered by the network cable itself so no separate power plug is needed at each camera location.
📍 Throughout building + exterior
Note: MR36H removed from plan

The original plan had 1x MR36H wall-plate AP for Office 118. It is replaced by a ceiling MR57 (AP-6) at the middle-right of the building. AP-6 covers both Office 118 and the right warehouse edge. Return the MR36H unit to the client as surplus.

Building Floor Plan

Study this before starting. Closet 104 (left side) is your main room — every cable runs back here.

Figure 1: Floor Plan — Scale 1/8" = 1' | Total area ~12,182 sq ft

Room / AreaRoom #What You Install
Server Closet — Main Rack Room104Rack, UPS, Router, Switch, MG21 — everything central
Lobby + Reception100 / 105AP-1 (ceiling) + Camera CAM-1 + Camera CAM-5
Conference Room114AP-2 (ceiling)
Office back cluster (102, Dispatch 106, Break Rm 108, Copy 113)MultipleAP-3 (one AP covers all) + CAM-4 + CAM-6
Middle-right of building (Office 118 area)118 areaAP-6 (ceiling MR57) + Camera CAM-3
Warehouse — open parking area117AP-4 (west) + AP-5 (east) + CAM-7 to CAM-12
Loading DockDockCAM-13 + CAM-14
Building ExteriorOutsideCAM-15, CAM-16, CAM-17, CAM-18
Office corridor (hallway)HallwayCAM-2

PART 1 — DRY RUN

Set up and test all rack devices in Closet 104. Nothing is bolted to walls yet. Fix any problem now — it is much easier before anything is mounted permanently.

Rule: Test first, install second.

Never mount a device permanently until it shows "Online" in the Meraki Dashboard. This one rule saves hours of frustration later.

DR-0  —  Preconditions  |  Do These Before Touching Any Hardware
STEP DR-0.1 Confirm Meraki Dashboard Access ▼
  • 1
    Connect your laptop to any working Wi-Fi or phone hotspot.
  • 2
    Open a web browser. Go to dashboard.meraki.com.
  • 3
    Log in with your Meraki credentials.
  • 4
    Confirm you can see the Meraki Dashboard home screen.
✅

CHECK: Meraki Dashboard loads successfully. If login fails, contact whoever gave you the account details before proceeding.

STEP DR-0.2 Buy All Missing Items Before Going to the Warehouse ▼
  • 1
    T-Mobile nano SIM — activated with 50GB+ business plan. Buy at any T-Mobile store.
  • 2
    Network cable — 3 spools of 1000 feet each (used to run from the main closet to every camera and Wi-Fi point in the building). Search: "CAT6 bulk cable 1000ft spool".
  • 3
    Patch panel x2 (a 24-port cable organiser that mounts in the rack). This is where every cable in the building plugs in at the Closet 104 end — it keeps things organised and makes tracing cables easy. Search: "24 port CAT6 patch panel".
  • 4
    Cable wall connectors x60 + wall socket covers x30. These are the small plastic pieces that go at the device end of each cable, fitted into a cover plate on the wall. Search: "CAT6 keystone jack" and "single gang wall plate".
  • 5
    Cable tester — a device that checks whether each cable you pulled is working correctly. It tells you PASS or FAIL. Never skip testing. Search: "Fluke MicroScanner cable tester".
  • 6
    Label maker. Search: "Brother P-touch label maker".
  • 7
    Short ready-made cables in various lengths (1 foot, 3 feet, 5 feet) and different colours. These are used to connect devices to each other inside the rack cabinet. Buy a mix of blue, yellow, red, and grey.
  • 8
    Metal tube (called conduit) — 3/4 inch diameter. This is the protective tube that all cables must run inside when going through the warehouse. Georgia law requires this. Buy at Home Depot or Lowes — ask for "3/4 inch EMT conduit".
  • 9
    Stud finder — a device that beeps when it detects a solid wood beam hidden inside a wall. You need this before drilling any holes in walls. Also buy a punch-down tool — a small hand tool used to push cable wires into the patch panel connectors.
  • 10
    Cable supports — J-hooks (small plastic hooks that hold cables up in ceiling spaces), cable staples (to fix cables along walls), and velcro ties (to bundle cables neatly together).
  • 11
    Rack filler panels x3 (flat blank panels that cover empty slots in the rack cabinet — they keep airflow moving correctly so devices do not overheat). Also buy 1 rack power strip (a power board designed to mount inside the rack) and 2 flat rack shelves.
  • 12
    Exterior silicone sealant (a rubber sealant in a tube — used to fill and seal holes through exterior walls so rain cannot get in). Also buy beam clamps — metal clips that attach the conduit tube to the steel warehouse beams.
✅

CHECK: Every item above is in your hands or confirmed on order before going to the warehouse.

STEP DR-0.3 Prepare Your Home Workspace ▼
Home dry run — no rack, no UPS, no MX68 needed.

You will set everything up on a table or the floor. All you need is a clear flat surface, your devices, and your home internet from Xfinity. The 20A outlet check is a warehouse-only step — it is in the Field-Only checklist (DR-4) at the end of the dry run.

  • 1
    Find a clear flat surface — a table or the floor. Large enough to lay out: 1 switch, 1 MG21, 2 APs, 1 camera.
  • 2
    Confirm your Xfinity XB8 home router is on and connected to the internet. This replaces the MX68 for the dry run.
  • 3
    Gather all available devices: MS125-48FP switch, MG21 5G gateway, 2x MR57 APs, 1x MV12W camera, and short patch cables.
✅

CHECK: Clear workspace ready, Xfinity XB8 is online, all dry run devices are in front of you.

STEP DR-0.4 Configure Meraki Dashboard — Do Online, No Hardware Needed ▼
⚠️

Rule 6 (Allow All) MUST be the very last firewall rule. If it is above any Deny rule, all security blocks are overridden.

Why configure before hardware?

Meraki devices download their settings from the cloud automatically when powered on. Configure first and devices self-configure the moment they connect.

  • 1
    Create your account: Go to dashboard.meraki.com in your browser. Click the organisation name at the top left. Select "Create new organization". Name it [ClientName]-HQ (replace ClientName with the actual client name). Country: United States. Click Save.
  • 2
    Create Network: In the dashboard go to Organization → Networks → Add network. Set the name to HQ-Network. For the type, select Combined — this is very important, do not choose anything else. Set the time zone to Eastern Time. Click Create.
  • 3
    Claim Devices: Organization → Inventory → Add devices → Enter serial number of each device (sticker on bottom or back). Assign all to HQ-Network. Devices: 1x MX68, 1x MG21, 1x MS125-48FP, 6x MR57, 18x MV12W.
  • 4
    Create the invisible network zones (VLANs): In the dashboard, go to: Security and SD-WAN → Addressing and VLANs → click Enable VLANs. Add 4 VLANs: Create these 4 zones: Zone 10 named CORP-OFFICE (address 10.0.10.1), Zone 20 named CORP-WAREHOUSE (address 10.0.20.1), Zone 30 named GUEST (address 10.0.30.1), Zone 40 named IOT-SECURITY (address 10.0.40.1). For each zone, set the internet address lookup servers (DNS) to: main = 8.8.8.8, backup = 1.1.1.1. These are Google's free public servers and are very reliable.
  • 5
    Security rules: In the dashboard go to In the dashboard go to Security and SD-WAN → Firewall → Layer 3 firewall rules (security rules). Add these 6 rules in this exact order (order matters — the system reads them top to bottom): Add these 6 rules in this exact order: (1) BLOCK — Guest zone cannot reach Staff zone (2) BLOCK — Guest zone cannot reach Warehouse zone (3) BLOCK — Guest zone cannot reach Camera zone (4) BLOCK — Camera zone cannot reach Staff zone (5) BLOCK — Camera zone cannot reach Warehouse zone (6) ALLOW — Everything else is allowed (this must always be the last rule)
  • 6
    SSIDs: Wireless → SSIDs. Set up these 4 Wi-Fi networks: — CORP-OFFICE: security type WPA3 — this is the strongest and most modern Wi-Fi password protection available, zone 10, broadcast on all 6 APs — CORP-WAREHOUSE: security type WPA2 — a reliable Wi-Fi password protection standard compatible with most older devices, zone 20, broadcast on AP-4 and AP-5 only (warehouse APs), 2.4 GHz frequency only — this is a wider-range Wi-Fi signal that travels further and works better with older warehouse devices — GUEST: security type WPA2 — a reliable Wi-Fi password protection standard compatible with most older devices, zone 30, all 6 APs, guest isolation ON — this means guests on the guest Wi-Fi cannot see each other's devices and cannot see any internal building device. Each guest is isolated. — IOT: security type WPA2 — a reliable Wi-Fi password protection standard compatible with most older devices, zone 40, all 6 APs, hidden — this network name does not appear in the Wi-Fi list when anyone searches for networks. Only devices that already know the exact name and password can connect. Turn off all remaining unused Wi-Fi slots.
  • 7
    Switch ports: In the dashboard go to Switch → Switch ports. Select each group of ports and apply the settings below: Ports 1–6 (Wi-Fi access points): type = Trunk (carries all network zones), cable power ON Ports 7–24 (cameras): type = Access, zone 40, cable power ON Ports 25–37 (computers/workstations): type = Access, zone 10, cable power OFF The SFP uplink port (a high-speed port on the end of the switch used only to connect to the router): set type to Trunk, all zones
✅

CHECK: Dashboard shows: organization created, network created, all devices claimed, 4 VLANs, firewall rules in correct order, 4 SSIDs configured, switch ports set.

DR-1  —  Home Table Setup
Home dry run — no rack cabinet, no UPS, no MX68.

Everything sits on a table or the floor. Your Xfinity XB8 home router provides internet. The MS125-48FP switch powers all devices via PoE through the cables. No separate power adapters are needed at each device.

Figure 2: Home dry run setup — XB8 provides internet, switch powers all devices via PoE

The rack build happens at the warehouse — not at home.

Click below to preview what the final rack will look like. You will build this physically in Closet 104 during the On Field phase.

Preview: What the warehouse rack will look like (click to expand)

Figure 2b: Final rack layout at warehouse — build this in Closet 104 on site

STEP DR-1.1 Connect XB8 to the Switch ▼
  • 1
    Place the MS125-48FP switch on your table. Plug its power cable into a normal wall outlet.
  • 2
    Take a short CAT6 patch cable. Plug one end into a LAN port on the back of your Xfinity XB8 router.
  • 3
    Plug the other end into any port on the MS125-48FP switch. This gives the switch access to your home internet.
  • 4
    Wait 2 minutes. Port LEDs on the front of the switch will light up.
✅

CHECK: Switch LEDs are lit. Go to dashboard.meraki.com — the MS125-48FP should show Online within 5 minutes.

STEP DR-1.2 Connect MG21, APs, and Camera to the Switch via PoE ▼
PoE = Power over Ethernet.

The switch sends both data AND power through the CAT6 cable. The moment you plug a cable from the switch into an MR57 AP or MV12W camera, the device powers on immediately. No separate power adapters needed at each device.

  • 1
    MG21: Plug a network cable from switch port 48 into the MG21 port. No SIM yet — that is fine. It will still power on and register to the dashboard.
  • 2
    MR57 AP #1: Plug a CAT6 patch cable from switch port 1 into the network cable port (the rectangular slot that the cable clicks into) on the back of the first MR57. It powers on immediately through the cable.
  • 3
    MR57 AP #2: Plug a CAT6 patch cable from switch port 2 into the second MR57.
  • 4
    MV12W Camera: Plug a CAT6 patch cable from switch port 7 into the camera network cable port (the rectangular slot that the cable clicks into). Camera powers on immediately.
  • 5
    Wait 5 minutes for all devices to fully boot and register to the Meraki cloud.
✅

CHECK: All devices powered on through the cable with no separate power adapters. If a device does not power on, check the patch cable is firmly clicked in at both ends.

STEP DR-1.3 Verify All Devices Are Online in Dashboard ▼
  • 1
    Open your laptop and go to dashboard.meraki.com. Log in.
  • 2
    Go to your HQ-Network. Check that the MS125-48FP switch shows green Online.
  • 3
    Check that MG21 shows Online. Note: it has no SIM so there is no 5G internet through it — but the device still registers to the dashboard. This is correct.
  • 4
    Go to Wireless → Access Points. Both MR57 units should show green Online.
  • 5
    Go to Cameras → Camera health. The MV12W should show Online with live video visible.
✅

CHECK: 5 devices total — MS125, MG21, 2x MR57, 1x MV12W — all show green Online. Live camera video is visible. If any device is offline after 10 minutes: check the patch cable is firmly clicked in at both ends of that device.

DR-2  —  Power On (In This Exact Order)
⚠️

Never power everything on at once. Wait the full time listed before turning on the next device.

Home dry run power-on is much simpler — no strict sequence needed.

At the warehouse you must follow a strict order because the UPS powers everything. At home each device has its own power adapter, so there is no critical sequence. The switch is already on from Step DR-1.1. Everything else powered on via PoE when you plugged in the cables.

DeviceHow it powers on at homeWhat to look for
MS125 SwitchPlugged into wall outlet in DR-1.1Port LEDs lit. Dashboard shows Online.
MG21 5GPowered via PoE from switch port 48LED lights up. Shows Online in dashboard. No internet (no SIM) — that is expected.
MR57 APs x2Powered via PoE from switch ports 1 and 2LED blinks white → solid white/green = Online.
MV12W CameraPowered via PoE from switch port 7LED blinks → solid = Online. Live video in dashboard.
MX68 RouterNot available at homeWill be added at the warehouse. Claimed in dashboard already.
UPSNot available at homeWill be installed at the warehouse.
✅

CHECK: Dashboard shows all 4 devices Online — MS125, MG21, 2x MR57, 1x MV12W. Yellow rows (MX68, UPS) are for the warehouse only — ignore them during home dry run.

DR-3  —  Dry Run Verification
STEP DR-3.1 Internet Test (no failover test at home) ▼
Important: IP address will look different at home.

At the warehouse, the MX68 assigns addresses like 10.0.10.x (VLAN 10). At home, your Xfinity XB8 assigns addresses like 192.168.x.x. This is expected — do not worry if you do not see 10.0.10.x at home.

  • 1
    Plug a short patch cable from any unused switch port into your laptop.
  • 2
    Check your IP address. Windows: open Command Prompt, type ipconfig. Mac: System Settings → Network. You should see 192.168.x.x — this is normal at home.
  • 3
    Open a browser, go to speedtest.net. Confirm internet works. You will see your Xfinity home speed — not the warehouse AT&T speed. That is fine.
⚠️

5G failover test CANNOT be done at home. Failover requires the MX68 router and an active SIM in the MG21. This test is in the Field-Only checklist (DR-4) — it will be done at the warehouse.

✅

CHECK: Your laptop has internet through the switch. Speedtest.net confirms it works. IP address starts with 192.168.x.x — this is correct for the home dry run.

STEP DR-3.2 Quick AP Test — One MR57 ▼
  • 1
    Take one MR57 out of its box.
  • 2
    Plug a short patch cable from switch port 1 to the network cable port (the rectangular slot that the cable clicks into) on the back of the MR57. It powers on through the cable — no separate plug needed.
  • 3
    Wait 3 minutes. LED will blink white during startup, then turn solid white or green = online.
  • 4
    On your phone, go to Wi-Fi settings and search for networks. You should see CORP-OFFICE, CORP-WAREHOUSE, and GUEST all appear in your Wi-Fi search list.
  • 5
    Connect your phone to CORP-OFFICE and confirm internet works.
✅

CHECK: All 3 Wi-Fi network names visible. CORP-OFFICE connects with working internet. Dashboard → Wireless → Access Points shows the MR57 as Online.

STEP DR-3.3 Quick Camera Test — One MV12W ▼
  • 1
    Take one MV12W camera out of its box.
  • 2
    Plug a short patch cable from switch port 7 (labeled CAM-1) to the network cable port (the rectangular slot that the cable clicks into) on the camera. It powers on through the cable.
  • 3
    Wait 2 minutes.
  • 4
    Go to dashboard.meraki.com → Cameras → Camera health. The camera should appear with a green dot and show live video.
✅

CHECK: Camera shows Online with live video. If offline after 5 minutes: Switch → Switch ports → find port 7 → confirm PoE is ON.

🎉 Home Dry Run Complete!

All checks passed = every device powers on and registers correctly. You are ready to go to the warehouse. Before you go, review the Field-Only Steps below — these are the things you could not test at home.

DR-4  —  Field-Only Steps  |  Cannot Be Done at Home — Do These at the Warehouse
⚠️

These steps were NOT tested during the home dry run. You must complete every one of them at the warehouse before the installation is finished.

StepWhat to DoWhy It Could Not Be Done at Home
Rack assemblyMount AR112 on wall, install UPS, PDU, shelves, patch panel, switch, MX68, MG21 in correct slotsNo rack cabinet or UPS available at home
20A outlet checkConfirm Closet 104 wall outlet is 20A and dedicatedWarehouse site visit required
UPS power sequencePower on in exact order: UPS → MX68 → MG21 → Switch, with correct wait timesNo UPS available at home
Connection to MX68 routerConnect MX68 to AT&T fiber and confirm main internet port (WAN1) is activeNo MX68 at home. AT&T fiber not at home.
Network zone isolation testConnect to GUEST Wi-Fi and run a ping test to 10.0.10.1 — it should get no reply, confirming guests are blocked from the staff networkRequires the MX68 router — the invisible zone walls only activate when the MX68 is connected
5G failover testUnplug AT&T, confirm internet switches to 5G within 40 secondsRequires active SIM in MG21 + MX68 + AT&T fiber
Internet speed testConfirm 800 Mbps+ download and upload on VLAN 10AT&T fiber not at home
Good news: your Meraki Dashboard configuration is already done.

The VLANs, firewall rules, SSIDs, and switch port settings you configured in DR-0.4 are saved in the cloud. When the MX68 powers on at the warehouse, it automatically downloads and activates all those settings. You do not need to configure anything again on site.

PART 2 — ON FIELD

Install devices room by room. Work through each room in order. Never close a ceiling or seal a wall until every cable in that area passes the cable tester.

OF-0  —  Cabling Rules  |  Read Before Pulling Any Cable
WhereHow to run the cableWhy — in plain English
Office rooms
Rooms with a false ceiling above (the removable white tiles)		Push the ceiling tile up and slide it aside. Feed the cable through the space above the tiles. Put the tile back when done. Use small plastic hooks (J-hooks) to hold the cable up every 4 to 5 feet so it does not sag.The ceiling tiles lift out easily with your hands — no tools needed. The space above is hollow and you can run cable through it without drilling any walls.
Warehouse
The large open area with steel beams visible above		The cable must travel inside a metal tube (called EMT conduit) that is clipped to the steel beams. The cable goes inside the tube — never left hanging in the open air.Georgia state law requires all cables in open warehouse spaces to be inside a protective metal tube. If an inspector sees bare cable hanging from beams, you will fail inspection. The tube also protects the cable from forklifts, falling objects, and accidental damage.
Outdoors
Any cable that goes through an exterior wall or is exposed to weather		Use outdoor-rated cable (a tougher cable with a special jacket). Run it through a weatherproof tube (conduit). Where the tube goes through the wall, fill the gap completely with silicone caulk — a rubber sealant you apply like toothpaste from a tube. Seal both the inside AND outside of the wall.Regular indoor cable is not built for rain, sunlight, or temperature changes. It will crack and stop working within a few months if left outside. The caulk stops rainwater from getting inside the building through the hole you drilled.
⚠️

NEVER leave CAT6 cable exposed in the warehouse. This is a Georgia building code violation.

Wire colour order — must be used at BOTH ends of every cable

T568B wiring colour order — memorise this, use at both ends of every cable

⚠️

Both ends of every cable MUST use T568B. One wrong end = cable fails tester = device does not work.

How to read the cable tester

Cable tester: PASS = all 8 lights blink in order. FAIL = re-punch and re-test.

OF-0.1 — Access Point Placement (Revised — 6 APs)

Figure 4: AP locations — Blue = Office (AP-1,2,3) | Orange = Warehouse (AP-4,5) | Purple = Right-side (AP-6)

OF-0.2 — How APs Connect to the Switch

Each Wi-Fi access point has its own individual cable running directly back to the switch in Closet 104. The access points are NOT connected to each other.

This is called star topology — every AP connects to the center switch in Closet 104. If one cable fails, only that AP goes down. The others stay up.

Switch (MS125-48FP) in Closet 104 │ ├── Port 1 ──── AP-1 (Lobby / Reception ceiling) ├── Port 2 ──── AP-2 (Conference Room 114 ceiling) ├── Port 3 ──── AP-3 (Office back — 102 / Dispatch / Break Rm / Copy) ├── Port 4 ──── AP-4 (Warehouse west — 1/3 from left wall, centered) ├── Port 5 ──── AP-5 (Warehouse east — 1/3 from right wall, centered) └── Port 6 ──── AP-6 (Middle right — covers Office 118 + warehouse edge)
QuestionAnswer
Are APs connected to each other?NO. Each AP has its own cable directly to the switch.
How many cables for APs?6 total — one per AP, switch ports 1–6.
How do APs get power?Power comes through the same network cable that carries data (called PoE — Power over Ethernet). No separate power plug is needed at each access point.
Which AP covers Office 118?AP-6 at middle-right of building. Also covers the right warehouse edge.
Why only 2 warehouse APs?Open car parking with no metal shelving or forklifts. MR57 covers 150–200ft in open space. Two APs cover the full floor for phones, tablets, and laptops.

OF-0.3 — Camera Placement Map

Figure 5: Camera locations — Red = Office | Orange = Warehouse + Dock | Green = Exterior

🚪 CLOSET 104 — Main Server Room

Install: Mount AR112 Rack Cabinet Permanently + All Rack Devices
Now you permanently build the full rack in Closet 104.

You practised connecting devices in the home dry run. Now install everything into the AR112 wall cabinet in the correct slot order — UPS at bottom (U11-U12), patch panel at top (U1). Follow the slot order carefully.

🎒 What to carry:
  • AR112 rack cabinet + all dry run devices
  • Stud finder
  • Drill with 5/16" bit
  • Lag bolts (included with AR112)
  • Bubble level (or phone level app)
STEP OF-104.1 Mount the AR112 Cabinet to the Wall ▼

Studs are hidden inside the wall — stud finder beeps when it detects one

⚠️

Never mount on drywall anchors alone. The loaded cabinet weighs 80+ lbs. All bolts must hit solid wood studs inside the wall.

  • 1
    Turn on the stud finder. Slide it slowly across the wall in Closet 104. When it beeps or lights up = solid stud (wood beam inside wall). Mark each stud with a pencil.
  • 2
    Hold the empty AR112 cabinet against the wall at about 5 feet height. Mark which mounting holes line up with the studs you found.
  • 3
    Set the cabinet down. Drill pilot holes 2.5 inches deep into the studs using a 5/16" drill bit.
  • 4
    Hold the cabinet back up. Insert and tighten all lag bolts — every single bolt must go into solid stud.
  • 5
    Place a bubble level on top of the cabinet. Adjust until perfectly level. Tighten all bolts fully.
  • 6
    Push and pull the cabinet firmly in all directions. It must not move at all.
STEP OF-104.2 Transfer Rack into Wall Cabinet ▼
  • 1
    Power everything off: press UPS button off, unplug all PDU devices.
  • 2
    Transfer all devices into the wall-mounted cabinet in the exact same slot order as the dry run.
  • 3
    Reconnect all internal patch cables exactly as done in Step DR-1.5.
  • 4
    Power on in the same order: UPS first → MX68 → MG21 → Switch.
✅

CHECK: MX68, MG21, and MS125 all show green Online in the Meraki Dashboard.

🏢 LOBBY (100) + RECEPTION (105)

Install: AP-1 (ceiling) + Camera CAM-1 (lobby) + Camera CAM-5 (reception)
🎒 What to carry:
  • 1x MR57 (AP-1)
  • 2x MV12W cameras (CAM-1, CAM-5)
  • Network cable + cable tester + punch-down tool (wire pressing tool)
  • Label maker + drill + J-hooks
STEP OF-100.1 Pull Cable and Mount AP-1 (Wi-Fi for Lobby/Reception) ▼

Ceiling tile: push up → tilt → slide free. Reverse to replace after installing cable.

MR57 T-bar bracket: clips onto ceiling grid. No drilling. Connect cable, rotate AP until locked.

  • 1
    Label one end of a new CAT6 cable: AP-1. Start at Patch Panel Port 1 in Closet 104.
  • 2
    Feed cable above the ceiling tiles from Closet 104 to the center of the lobby/reception ceiling. Use J-hooks every 4–5 feet to support the cable.
  • 3
    At the AP location, cut a small clean hole in the ceiling tile for the cable. Drop the cable through — leave 12 inches (about 30 centimetres) of extra cable hanging at the device location — this gives you enough slack to connect it comfortably.
  • 4
    At the Closet 104 end of the cable, push each of the 8 coloured wires into Patch Panel Port 1. Push them in using the T568B colour order shown in the diagram above. Use your punch-down tool to press each wire firmly into its slot — you will hear a small click.
  • 5
    Label both ends: AP-1. Test with cable tester: main unit into Port 1, remote into AP-1 cable end. Must show PASS.
  • 6
    Attach MR57 mounting bracket to the T-bar ceiling grid — clips hook on, no drilling needed.
  • 7
    Connect the AP-1 cable to the MR57 network cable port (the rectangular slot that the cable clicks into) — push until it clicks.
  • 8
    Connect Patch Panel Port 1 to Switch Port 1 in Closet 104.
  • 9
    Rotate MR57 clockwise onto the bracket until it locks. Replace ceiling tile.
✅

CHECK: AP-1 shows green Online in dashboard within 5 minutes. LED on the AP turns solid white or green.

STEP OF-100.2 Pull Cable and Mount CAM-1 (Lobby) and CAM-5 (Reception) ▼

Dome camera assembly: mount base → aim lens → snap dome on. Cable provides both power and data.

  • 1
    CAM-1 — Lobby Entrance: Position to cover the full entrance door — no blind spots. Pull CAT6 from Patch Panel Port 7.
  • 2
    Label both ends: CAM-1. Test — must show PASS.
  • 3
    Hold the MV12W mounting base at the ceiling. Mark screw holes. Mount with toggle bolts through the tile or T-bar clips.
  • 4
    Thread the cable through the center hole of the mounting base. Connect to the network cable port (the rectangular slot that the cable clicks into) inside the base — push until it clicks.
  • 5
    Screw the base to the ceiling. Snap the dome cover on — twist to lock.
  • 6
    Connect Port 7 to Switch Port 7 in Closet 104.
  • 7
    CAM-5 — Reception Desk: Position to cover the desk and the approach to it. Pull from Port 11. Label both ends: CAM-5. Test PASS. Mount same way.
  • 8
    Connect Port 11 to Switch Port 11 in Closet 104.
✅

CHECK: CAM-1 and CAM-5 both Online in dashboard. Live video on each confirms correct area with no blind spots.

🚶 MAIN OFFICE CORRIDOR (Hallway)

Install: Camera CAM-2
🎒 What to carry:
  • 1x MV12W camera (CAM-2)
  • Network cable + tools (tester, punch-down tool, labels, drill, J-hooks)
STEP OF-COR.1 Pull Cable and Mount CAM-2 (Corridor Camera) ▼
  • 1
    Position the camera at the end of the main office corridor, looking down the full hallway length.
  • 2
    Pull CAT6 from Patch Panel Port 8 (CAM-2) to the corridor ceiling location.
  • 3
    Label both ends: CAM-2. Test — must show PASS.
  • 4
    Mount camera. Connect Port 8 to Switch Port 8 in Closet 104.
✅

CHECK: CAM-2 Online. Live video shows full corridor length with no blind spots.

📊 CONFERENCE ROOM 114

Install: AP-2 (ceiling Wi-Fi)
🎒 What to carry:
  • 1x MR57 (AP-2)
  • Network cable + tools (tester, punch-down tool, labels, drill, J-hooks)
STEP OF-114.1 Pull Cable and Mount AP-2 (Conference Room Wi-Fi) ▼
  • 1
    Pull CAT6 from Patch Panel Port 2 (AP-2) to the ceiling center of Conference Room 114.
  • 2
    Label both ends: AP-2. Test — must show PASS.
  • 3
    Mount MR57 bracket on T-bar. Connect cable. Rotate AP until locked.
  • 4
    Connect Port 2 to Switch Port 2 in Closet 104.
✅

CHECK: AP-2 Online within 5 minutes. Connect your phone inside the conference room to CORP-OFFICE — internet works.

🖥️ OFFICE 102 + DISPATCH 106 + BREAK ROOM 108 + COPY/STORAGE 113

Install: AP-3 (one AP covers all 4 rooms) + Camera CAM-4 (Dispatch) + Camera CAM-6 (Break Room)
One AP covers this entire back office cluster.

These 4 rooms total about 1,500–2,000 sq ft. Interior walls are thin partitions — Wi-Fi passes through easily. One MR57 centered above this cluster covers all rooms.

🎒 What to carry:
  • 1x MR57 (AP-3)
  • 2x MV12W cameras (CAM-4, CAM-6)
  • Network cable + tools (tester, punch-down tool, labels, drill, J-hooks)
STEP OF-BACK.1 Pull Cable and Mount AP-3 (Office Back Wi-Fi) ▼

AP-3 covers all four back office rooms — Wi-Fi passes through thin interior partition walls.

  • 1
    Position AP-3 on the ceiling centered between Dispatch (106) and Break Room (108) — this one AP covers all four back rooms.
  • 2
    Pull CAT6 from Patch Panel Port 3 (AP-3). Label: AP-3. Test — must show PASS.
  • 3
    Mount MR57 bracket. Connect cable. Rotate until locked.
  • 4
    Connect Port 3 to Switch Port 3 in Closet 104.
✅

CHECK: AP-3 Online. Walk into all four rooms (102, 106, 108, 113) and confirm strong Wi-Fi signal on your phone in each room.

STEP OF-BACK.2 Pull Cable and Mount CAM-4 and CAM-6 ▼
  • 1
    CAM-4 — Dispatch (106): Position to cover the full dispatch desk and room entrance. Pull from Port 10. Label: CAM-4. Test PASS. Mount. Connect Port 10 to Switch Port 10.
  • 2
    CAM-6 — Break Room (108): Position to cover the food area and entrance. Pull from Port 12. Label: CAM-6. Test PASS. Mount. Connect Port 12 to Switch Port 12.
✅

CHECK: CAM-4 and CAM-6 both Online. Live video confirms the correct area for each camera.

🏠 MIDDLE-RIGHT OF BUILDING (covers Office 118 + right warehouse edge)

Install: AP-6 (ceiling MR57) + Camera CAM-3
AP-6 is NOT mounted inside Office 118 — it goes at the middle of the right wall.

This central position covers Office 118 above and the right warehouse edge below. One AP doing two jobs. Same MR57 model as all other APs.

🎒 What to carry:
  • 1x MR57 (AP-6)
  • 1x MV12W camera (CAM-3)
  • Network cable + tools (tester, punch-down tool, labels, drill, J-hooks)
STEP OF-118.1 Pull Cable and Mount AP-6 and CAM-3 ▼

AP-6 at middle-right covers Office 118 above and right warehouse edge below. One AP, two zones.

  • 1
    AP-6 position: Find the ceiling position halfway up the right side of the building — where the office block meets the warehouse wall. This is the middle-right of the building, not inside Office 118.
  • 2
    Pull CAT6 from Patch Panel Port 6 (AP-6) through the ceiling to this position. Label: AP-6. Test — must show PASS.
  • 3
    Mount MR57 bracket on ceiling T-bar or beam. Connect cable. Rotate AP until locked.
  • 4
    Connect Port 6 to Switch Port 6 in Closet 104.
  • 5
    CAM-3 — Office 118 corridor: Position at the entry point of the Office 118 corridor. Note: this is a longer cable run — make sure you have enough cable.
  • 6
    Pull from Port 9 (CAM-3). Label: CAM-3. Test — must show PASS. Mount camera.
  • 7
    Connect Port 9 to Switch Port 9 in Closet 104.
✅

CHECK: AP-6 Online. Strong Wi-Fi signal inside Office 118 AND at the right edge of the warehouse. CAM-3 Online showing the Office 118 entry clearly.

🏭 WAREHOUSE 117 — Open Parking Area

Install: AP-4 (west) + AP-5 (east) + Cameras CAM-7 to CAM-12
2 APs are confirmed sufficient for this warehouse.

Open car parking — no metal shelving or forklifts. MR57 covers 150–200ft in this clean open environment. Staff use phones, tablets, and laptops. Two well-positioned APs cover the full floor including the loading dock.

⚠️

ALL warehouse cables MUST be inside 3/4" EMT metal conduit. No exposed cable. Georgia building code.

🎒 What to carry:
  • 2x MR57 (AP-4, AP-5)
  • 6x MV12W cameras (CAM-7 to CAM-12)
  • EMT conduit + beam clamps
  • Network cable + tools (tester, punch-down tool, labels, drill, J-hooks) + ladder
STEP OF-WH.1 Mount EMT Conduit Along Steel Beams ▼

EMT conduit on I-beam cross section: clamp attaches to the web (vertical part). Cable runs inside.

⚠️

Use a ladder safely at all times. Have a second person hold the ladder. Never climb on shelving or racking. Warehouse beams can be 20+ feet high.

  • 1
    Plan your conduit route first: stand under the beams and trace a path from the wall where cables enter from Closet 104, across the beams to each AP and camera location.
  • 2
    Mount beam clamps on the steel beams along your planned route — one clamp every 10 feet.
  • 3
    Thread the EMT conduit through the beam clamps and tighten the clamps firmly.
  • 4
    Add conduit fittings at corners and junctions.
STEP OF-WH.2 Pull All Warehouse Cables Inside the Conduit ▼
  • 1
    Label each cable before pulling: AP-4, AP-5, CAM-7 through CAM-12.
  • 2
    Pull each cable from Closet 104 through the conduit to the correct device location. Leave 12 inches of cable at each device location.
  • 3
    At the Closet 104 end of each cable, push the 8 coloured wires into the correct patch panel port following the T568B colour order from the diagram. Use your punch-down tool to press each wire into its slot firmly.
  • 4
    Test EVERY cable with the cable tester before mounting any device. Write down the result for each cable.
✅

CHECK: All 8 warehouse cables (AP-4, AP-5, CAM-7 to CAM-12) show PASS on the tester. Written record complete.

STEP OF-WH.3 Mount AP-4 and AP-5 on Steel Beams ▼

AP positioning rule: 1/3 from each end, centered. Never in corners — coverage gaps form there.

  • 1
    Position AP-4 one-third from the left wall, centered north-to-south in the warehouse. Do NOT mount in the corner.
  • 2
    Position AP-5 one-third from the right wall, centered north-to-south. Do NOT mount in the corner.
  • 3
    For each AP: attach the MR57 adjustable arm to the bracket. Clamp the arm to the steel beam using a beam clamp. Tighten firmly — the AP must not wobble.
  • 4
    Connect the CAT6 cable from the conduit to the MR57 network cable port (the rectangular slot that the cable clicks into). Rotate AP onto bracket until locked.
  • 5
    Connect Port 4 to Switch Port 4 (AP-4) and Port 5 to Switch Port 5 (AP-5) in Closet 104.
|------------ WAREHOUSE (~150ft wide x ~100ft deep) ------------| | | | AP-4 (•) AP-5 (•) | | (1/3 from left) (1/3 from right) | | | |_______________________ LOADING DOCK __________________________|
✅

CHECK: AP-4 and AP-5 both Online. Walk to all 4 warehouse corners and the loading dock — confirm strong Wi-Fi signal everywhere on your phone.

STEP OF-WH.4 Mount Warehouse Cameras CAM-7 to CAM-12 ▼

Camera grid: 6 cameras on beams, aimed at floor. Zones overlap for full warehouse coverage.

  • 1
    CAM-7 — Warehouse NW quadrant, on beam, aimed at floor below.
  • 2
    CAM-8 — Warehouse NE quadrant, on beam, aimed at floor below.
  • 3
    CAM-9 — Warehouse center-North, on beam, aimed down across center.
  • 4
    CAM-10 — Warehouse center-South, on beam, aimed down across center.
  • 5
    CAM-11 — Warehouse SE quadrant.
  • 6
    CAM-12 — Warehouse SW quadrant.
  • 7
    For each: clamp adjustable arm to beam. Connect cable. Angle camera to cover the floor area below.
  • 8
    Connect Patch Panel Ports 13–18 to Switch Ports 13–18 in Closet 104.
✅

CHECK: All 6 warehouse cameras (CAM-7 to CAM-12) Online. Live video shows good floor coverage with no large uncovered areas.

🚛 LOADING DOCK

Install: Cameras CAM-13 + CAM-14
🎒 What to carry:
  • 2x MV12W cameras (CAM-13, CAM-14)
  • Network cable + tools (tester, punch-down tool, labels, drill, J-hooks)
STEP OF-DOCK.1 Pull Cable and Mount CAM-13 and CAM-14 ▼

CAM-14 = dock door (captures arrivals). CAM-13 = dock interior (captures loading activity).

Why two cameras for the dock?

One camera cannot cover both angles simultaneously. CAM-14 faces the door to capture every vehicle arriving and leaving. CAM-13 faces inward to capture loading and unloading activity on the dock floor.

  • 1
    CAM-13 — Dock Interior: Position to cover where vehicles park and goods are loaded/unloaded. Pull from Port 19. Label: CAM-13. Test PASS. Mount. Connect Port 19 to Switch Port 19.
  • 2
    CAM-14 — Dock Entry: Position to cover the dock door and vehicle approach. Pull from Port 20. Label: CAM-14. Test PASS. Mount. Connect Port 20 to Switch Port 20.
✅

CHECK: CAM-13 and CAM-14 both Online. Live video shows dock interior and dock entry clearly.

🌧️ BUILDING EXTERIOR

Install: Cameras CAM-15, CAM-16, CAM-17, CAM-18
⚠️

Use outdoor-rated CAT6 and connectors. Seal every wall hole with silicone caulk inside AND outside. Unsealed holes cause serious water damage.

CameraLocationWhat It Covers
CAM-15Exterior frontMain entrance + front parking
CAM-16Exterior front rightRight side of front approach
CAM-17Exterior rearRear parking + back of building
CAM-18Exterior rear east cornerEast side rear approach
STEP OF-EXT.1 Pull Cable and Mount Exterior Cameras CAM-15 to CAM-18 ▼

Wall penetration: seal caulk on BOTH sides — inside and outside. One missed side = water damage.

Why outdoor-rated CAT6?

Indoor cable degrades in UV light, temperature swings, and moisture. Outdoor-rated cable has a UV-resistant jacket. Indoor cable outside will crack and fail within months even if it initially works.

⚠️

Use outdoor-rated CAT6 cable and connectors for all exterior runs. Seal every wall penetration with silicone caulk on both sides.

  • 1
    Plan the cable route from inside the building through the wall to each camera mount location.
  • 2
    Drill a hole through the exterior wall just large enough for the conduit to pass through.
  • 3
    Pass weatherproof conduit through the hole. Apply silicone caulk around the conduit on BOTH sides of the wall (inside and outside).
  • 4
    Pull outdoor-rated CAT6 cable through the conduit from Closet 104 to each camera location.
  • 5
    Mount cameras under an overhang where possible to reduce direct rain exposure.
  • 6
    Use outdoor-rated cable connectors (connectors designed to resist rain and weather) at each camera end.
  • 7
    Label all cables and test each — must show PASS.
  • 8
    Connect Ports 21–24 to Switch Ports 21–24 in Closet 104.
✅

CHECK: All 4 exterior cameras (CAM-15 to CAM-18) Online. Live video shows correct exterior areas. All wall holes fully sealed with silicone caulk — no gaps.

Final Testing — All Tests Must Pass Before Handover

Run every test. Write PASS or FAIL. Do not hand over until all pass.

Test 1
Internet speed (after AT&T activates)
How: Plug your laptop into a switch port in the rack. Open a browser and go to speedtest.net.
Pass: 800 Mbps+ download AND upload
Test 2
5G failover
How: On Windows: open Command Prompt and type: ping -t 8.8.8.8 then press Enter. On Mac: open Terminal and type: ping 8.8.8.8. You will see one line appear every second — this means internet is working. Then unplug the AT&T fiber cable.
Pass: Internet returns within 40 seconds automatically
Test 3
Automatic failback
How: Plug the AT&T fiber cable back in after you completed the previous test.
Pass: Returns to AT&T within 2 minutes, no action needed
Test 4
Wi-Fi signal everywhere
How: Walk through every room and area of the building with your phone. Search for Wi-Fi and check the signal bars. Go to all 4 corners of the warehouse and the loading dock.
Pass: Strong signal in all offices, all warehouse corners, loading dock
Test 5
Guest network isolation
How: Connect your phone or laptop to the GUEST Wi-Fi network. Then on a laptop open Command Prompt (Windows) or Terminal (Mac) and type: ping 10.0.10.1 and press Enter.
Pass condition: you get NO reply — the ping just times out with no response. This is the correct and expected result. It means the firewall is working and guests are correctly blocked from the staff network. A failure here would be if you DO get a reply.
Test 6
All cameras online
How: Dashboard → Cameras → count all
Pass: 18/18 cameras Online with live video
Test 7
Camera coverage
How: Walk in front of every camera, check live feed
Pass: All entry doors visible, no blind spots at any camera
Test 8
Camera playback
How: In the Meraki Dashboard, click on any camera. At the bottom of the screen you will see a timeline. Click somewhere on the timeline to go back in time, then press play. Watch the footage play back.
Pass: Footage plays clearly
Test 9
All APs online
How: Dashboard → Wireless → Access Points
Pass: 6/6 APs Online
Test 10
Workstation network
How: Plug laptop into any switch port numbered 25 to 37. Check your IP address — on Windows: open Command Prompt and type ipconfig. On Mac: go to System Settings → Network.
Pass condition: your IP address starts with 10.0.10 — for example 10.0.10.45. This confirms the laptop is on the correct staff network zone.

Quick Troubleshooting

If something does not work, check here first.

Device shows Offline after 10 minutes
Plugged into PDU? PDU plugged into UPS output? UPS turned on?
AP Online but Wi-Fi network name not visible
SSID enabled in dashboard? AP included in that SSID's broadcast list?
Camera not appearing in dashboard
Is cable power (PoE) turned on for that switch port? In the dashboard go to Switch → Switch ports, find the camera's port number, and confirm PoE is ON. Also check: is the camera's serial number registered under Organization → Inventory?
Cable tester shows FAIL
T568B colour order at both ends? Re-punch wires with punch-down tool. Check for bent or broken wires.
IP address shows 169.254.x.x (a self-assigned address meaning no network found)
The device tried to get a network address but failed. Check in the dashboard that the correct network zone number is assigned to that switch port. Also check that automatic address assignment is turned on for that zone in the MX68 router settings — go to Security and SD-WAN → Addressing and VLANs.
Guest Wi-Fi can reach internal devices
The security rules are in the wrong order. In the dashboard, go to Security and SD-WAN → Firewall. Check that all the BLOCK rules appear ABOVE the Allow All rule. The system reads rules from top to bottom — if Allow All is first, nothing gets blocked.
5G failover not triggering
Is the MG21 showing Online in the dashboard? Go to Security and SD-WAN → SD-WAN settings — is the backup internet set to "Failover" mode (not "Load Balance")? Is the T-Mobile SIM card activated with an active data plan?
PoE device (camera or AP) not powering on
Is cable power (PoE) turned on for that switch port in the dashboard? Check that the total power drawn by all cable-powered devices does not exceed 740 watts — this is the maximum the switch can supply.
Weak signal in Office 118
Move AP-6 slightly toward the Office 118 side of its current mount position.
Weak signal at warehouse edge or corner
Check that AP-4 and AP-5 are at the 1/3-from-each-end position — not in corners.

Every cable in the building connects back to these ports in Closet 104.

Installation Sign-Off Checklist

Tick every item before telling the client the job is done.

🎉 All items complete — ready for client handover!
